Selene Huang: yeah, for `tools-json-strict-v3` our guardrail is max -1.0pp on JSON validity (and no new failure mode clusters). -3.6pp is a hard block if it holds up.

Sana Farid: do we know n + CI? want to sanity check significance.

Arjun Desai: n=2,000 prompts sampled (same seed as last week). bootstrap CI for delta is [-4.4pp, -2.7pp]. so… not noise.

Karthik Iyer: is regression isolated to int8 profile? do we see it in fp8 mix?

Arjun Desai: tested `qpl_llama3_70b_fp8_w_fp8_a_fp16_v0.2` quickly (smaller run, n=600) and JSON validity delta was -0.4pp (within noise). savings are smaller though (~-8% cost/token).

Jonas Weber: do we have per-length breakdown? tool calls often have longer contexts.

Arjun Desai: yep, it’s worse at context >4k.
- <=4k: -2.1pp
- >4k: -6.8pp
Also seeing more “partial tool args” where it ends early.

Mateo Alvarez: (runtime side) any chance this is not “quality” but a truncation / max_tokens interaction? e.g. different tokenization length under quant or earlier EOS?

Arjun Desai: good q. we controlled max_tokens and verified output token counts. it’s not systematically shorter; it’s more malformed JSON.

Olivia Grant: is this the suite that uses the strict validator + whitespace normalization? could be formatting drift.

Arjun Desai: validator is strict JSON parse after whitespace normalization only. commas/braces are real errors.

Selene Huang: this is exactly the sort of suite we need to be conservative on. if customers enable quant on tool routes and JSON breaks, it’s a bad beta story.

Karthik Iyer: hypotheses?

Arjun Desai: 2 likely causes:
1) calibration set under-represents tool-calling / JSON-heavy outputs (more natural language)
2) w8a8 is pushing logit noise on punctuation tokens (comma/brace) esp in long context

Jonas Weber: we should probably mark v0.4 as “do not publish” until we fix. do we have an alternative int8 variant? maybe w8a16?

Arjun Desai: we tried `w8a16` earlier; savings drop to ~-10% but quality was fine on tools suite (n=800, delta -0.7pp). not fully rerun yet.

Sana Farid: if we’re going to compare variants, we should run paired eval w/ same prompt sample and do McNemar on the binary pass/fail, not just bootstrap. can help decide quickly.

Selene Huang: +1. also cluster failures—if it’s the same 50 prompts failing, that’s less scary than broad drift.

Arjun Desai: failures are broad. not just a handful.

Dr. Maya Srinivasan: ok action-wise: don’t publish `v0.4` to the library. treat as blocked. can we spin a `v0.5` with tool-heavy calibration? and in parallel rerun `w8a16` as a fallback candidate.

Arjun Desai: yep. I can:
- build `calset-2025-02-20-toolsmix` (pull from `tools-json-strict-v3` + some customer-ish tool traces)
- recalibrate int8 and rerun full suite tonight

Jonas Weber: pls also run perf suite on A100/H100 for the w8a16 candidate so we have something shippable if v0.5 slips.

Mateo Alvarez: if we end up recommending w8a16 for tool routes, we should make sure runtime selection + compatibility matrix can express “tooling-safe” caveat. otherwise customers will pick the biggest savings one.

Selene Huang: we can add a caveat field: “Not recommended for tool/structured output routes” for now, but I’d prefer we just not publish until it passes.

Olivia Grant: for beta gating: should we add `tools-json-strict-v3` as a REQUIRED suite for any profile marked “general purpose”? currently it’s required for chat models but not embeddings obviously.

Dr. Maya Srinivasan: yes. make it required for any instruct/chat model profile, especially anything intended for route-level enablement. Arjun/Selene pls sync and update the eval gating doc.

Sana Farid: also note this in the weekly results sheet w/ a clear “BLOCKED” label so no one accidentally flips “recommended”.

Arjun Desai: will do. summary:
- `qpl_llama3_70b_int8_w8a8_v0.4` blocked due to -3.6pp JSON validity on `tools-json-strict-v3` (worse >4k ctx)
- rerun w/ tool-heavy calibration as `v0.5`
- rerun `w8a16` as potential beta-safe option

Jonas Weber: thx. pls drop links to artifacts when you have them.

Arjun Desai: will post results + failure examples tomorrow AM.

Selene Huang: also please include a few raw failing outputs in the thread (redacted). helps when we explain to PM why we blocked.

Arjun Desai: yup.

Mateo Alvarez: (cc runtime) once you have candidate v0.5, we can do a quick perf-canary run to confirm no weird decode latency spikes.

Arjun Desai: great, I’ll ping you when the new artifacts land.

source.messages:
Selene Huang: trying to pin down what we mean by a “quality regression alert” for route experiments. we need something we can defend + implement w/o full stat-sig in GA. can we align on trigger conditions + how it shows up in Console?

Jonas Weber: +1. we have eval score streams per {experiment_id, variant_id} from eval harness, but we need: (a) baseline definition (control?) (b) normalization (scores across suites) (c) min sample size / windowing.

Olivia Grant: reminder: the current hook gives us per-prompt outputs w/ a score vector (one per eval rubric) + an overall “quality_index” we can compute. but “overall” is still a product choice.

Dr. Maya Srinivasan: my preference: make “quality regression” conservative + explainable. no p-values. do thresholding on a normalized aggregate + require minimum coverage. also avoid calling it “bad model” — it’s “quality signal suggests regression”.

Marissa Cole: from product/Console perspective we need something that fits the guardrails pattern next to latency/error/cost. so: a single status (healthy/degraded) + details drill-down.

Logan Wright: telemetry side: we can carry dimensions, but please keep cardinality bounded. if “quality” is a separate pipeline, the Console just needs: current value, delta vs control, sample count, last updated.

Selene Huang: ok, proposal v0:
- define control variant = variant with allocation “control=true” (or first variant if not set)
- compute Quality Score = quality_index normalized to 0-100 per eval suite, then aggregate as weighted average (default weights = 1 unless specified)
- alert triggers if delta_vs_control <= -X AND sample_count >= N AND eval_coverage >= C%
question: pick X/N/C.

Jonas Weber: control flag is good. for normalization: we can do per-suite z-score then map to 0-100, but that’s hard to explain. would rather keep it simple:
- quality_index already in [0,1] in harness output for most rubrics
- we can scale to [0,100]
- if a suite is “lower is better” we invert in harness once.

Olivia Grant: fwiw harness PR-812 output already includes a `higher_is_better` bit + we can standardize direction. so Console sees comparable numbers.

Alicia Perez: UX note: we need to show when we *don’t know*. e.g. “Insufficient eval data” state. otherwise customers will interpret tiny blips as regression.

Rafael Mendes: from SRE/guardrails: also need to define severity + whether it triggers auto-stop (probably not by default). we already agreed initial guardrails are soft-stop. quality should be “notify + banner” only.

Marissa Cole: yes. GA promise: quality guardrail exists, but it does NOT auto-stop unless customer explicitly enables (future). for now it contributes to “Experiment health: Degraded” with reason.

Selene Huang: +1. so for trigger values: I’m thinking:
- N = at least 200 eval’d requests per variant in the last 6h OR 50 in last 1h (whichever first)
- C = at least 80% of the configured prompt set completed in window
- X = drop of >= 3.0 points on 0-100 scale (absolute), OR >= 5% relative drop if baseline is low?

Jonas Weber: careful w/ absolute threshold only. if baseline is 95, -3 is meaningful. if baseline is 60, -3 could be noise. but we’re explicitly not doing significance. maybe combine absolute + relative:
Trigger if (delta_abs <= -3) AND (delta_rel <= -0.03)  (i.e., at least 3 points and 3% down).

Dr. Maya Srinivasan: agree w/ combined gating. also add a “cooldown” / “two consecutive windows” requirement to reduce flapping.

Logan Wright: windowing is important. if you do “two consecutive windows” pick a fixed interval (e.g., 30m). telemetry aggregation can support that.

Olivia Grant: note eval sampling isn’t 100% of traffic. for small allocations we might not hit N quickly. so we need the “insufficient data” case and *don’t* show a red badge prematurely.

Alicia Perez: can we model it like:
- Quality: Healthy (green)
- Quality: Degraded (red)
- Quality: Insufficient data (gray)
- Quality: Not configured (gray)
and tooltips that explain exactly what’s required.

Marissa Cole: yes. and in results view: show a mini card “Quality vs Control” with sparkline, delta, sample count, and “last eval run” timestamp.

Selene Huang: what about multiple eval suites? customers may attach different prompt sets to variants.

Jonas Weber: for v1, I think we require the prompt set is configured at experiment-level (same for all variants) to keep comparability. if variant-specific prompt sets, we should mark as “non-comparable” and not compute delta. (or compute but scary).

Marissa Cole: PRD assumes same eval suite per experiment for now, I’m ok to codify that in validation.

Rafael Mendes: +1. comparable signals only. otherwise support will get wrecked.

Selene Huang: ok, draft definition:
Quality regression alert (Console guardrail) triggers when:
1) Experiment has eval suite configured (prompt set + scoring config) and is comparable across variants.
2) For a variant, within the evaluation window (rolling 6h, refreshed every 30m):
   - eval_sample_count >= 200 (or >= 50 in last 1h for early warning)
   - coverage >= 80% of prompt set (or “coverage >= 40%” for early warning?)
3) Variant’s aggregated Quality Score delta vs control meets BOTH:
   - absolute drop >= 3 points (0-100)
   - relative drop >= 3%
4) Condition holds for 2 consecutive evaluation ticks (to avoid flapping)
Action: mark guardrail as Degraded, emit notification event, but do NOT auto-stop.

Jonas Weber: I’d avoid the 40% coverage early warning, it’s going to be noisy. maybe do early warning solely on sample count (>=50) but still require coverage >=80%. otherwise a few easy prompts might bias the score.

Dr. Maya Srinivasan: agree. coverage requirement is about representativeness. keep it.

Logan Wright: implementation detail: store both sample_count and prompt_count_completed to compute coverage. also store baseline score + variant score so Console can show numbers.

Alicia Perez: wording suggestion for UI:
- “Quality Degraded”
- Subtitle: “Quality score decreased by 4.2 (4.5%) vs control over last 6h (n=312 evals).”
- Secondary: “Eval coverage: 92% of prompt set. Updated 12m ago.”

Marissa Cole: love that. also add a “View details” -> table with per-suite deltas so people can see *why*.

Olivia Grant: per-suite deltas are feasible: e.g. `helpfulness`, `factuality`, `format_adherence`. just keep the default view on the aggregate.

Rafael Mendes: for guardrail thresholds: make X and relative threshold configurable? or hard-coded for beta?

Marissa Cole: beta: hard-coded defaults, maybe behind “advanced settings” later. customers already have too many knobs.

Selene Huang: ok. defaults baked for beta/GA v1, but we expose the thresholds in docs.

Jonas Weber: one more constraint: control score has to be “stable enough” too. if control n is tiny, delta is meaningless. so apply N/coverage gating to BOTH control + variant before computing delta.

Selene Huang: good catch. add that.

Logan Wright: +1. also we should version the definition so if thresholds change later we can annotate historical alerts.

Marissa Cole: action items:
- @Selene Huang write up this definition in the quality guardrails doc + send to me for UX copy
- @Olivia Grant confirm harness provides higher_is_better + suite-level outputs consistently
- @Logan Wright ensure results API can carry fields: aggregate_score, delta_abs, delta_rel, n, coverage, window_start/end, last_updated

Selene Huang: will do. I’ll add explicit “Insufficient data” state when gating not met (n < N or coverage < 80%).

Dr. Maya Srinivasan: :thumbsup: and please add a note: “not a statistical significance test” and “interpret with context”. we should be transparent.

Alicia Perez: :+1: I’ll mock the guardrail card w/ the copy above and include the 3-state status.

Rafael Mendes: thanks all. once this is in, SRE can decide if/when we page on quality (probably never) but we can route to Slack notifications channel.

Selene Huang: cool. I’ll post a draft in confluence later today and link it here.

source.messages:
Aditya Rao: We’re starting to see nice $/1M output token wins from the new quant profiles, but I’m nervous we’ll “win cost” and silently regress output quality. Can we tie `quantization_profile` to a quality guardrail hook (evals / canary) so we don’t ship surprises?

Quentin Harris: +1. Right now `quantization_profile` is just a tag in request metadata. No semantic meaning beyond “what runtime requested.” We need an authoritative mapping: (model_id, model_version, quant_profile) -> “guardrail suite + thresholds + last pass timestamp”.

Nadia Rahman: From telemetry side: we can emit quant_profile reliably (ENG-8425), but we can’t explode cardinality with freeform values. If we’re going to hook guardrails, we should enforce an allowed-value list and version it (e.g., `fp16`, `int8_w8a8_v2`, `gptq_int4_v1`, etc). Then eval system can key off it.

Aditya Rao: Exactly. Ideally:
1) Every quant_profile we expose in routing must have a guardrail policy.
2) If no policy, it shows as “UNGUARDED” in the unit econ dashboard and we block auto-rollouts.

Logan Wright: What do we mean by “hook” concretely? Are we trying to:
- gate the profile in prod (hard block), or
- just annotate the dashboard with “quality risk / last evaluated”, or
- alert when cost improves but quality dips?

Aditya Rao: v1: annotate + alert. v1.1: gating / rollout policy integration.

Marissa Cole: For Console, I can add a small badge next to quant profile (e.g., “Guardrailed: ✅” / “Unknown: ⚠️”), but I need a single API to fetch status. We should avoid the UI calling into eval systems directly.

Quentin Harris: We can create a small table/view in warehouse that the dashboard already hits:
`quality_guardrails_quant_profile_status`
Columns:
- `model_canonical_id`
- `model_version`
- `quantization_profile`
- `suite_name`
- `last_run_ts`
- `status` (pass/fail/unknown)
- `metric_summary` (string blob / link)

Nadia Rahman: If we do that, please be careful: don’t join per-request in aggregation. Join at query time or pre-aggregate hourly at the same grain we use for the dashboard (model/version/quant/region/tier). Otherwise we’ll introduce a ton of skew.

Logan Wright: Agree. Also “pass/fail” needs nuance. Some evals are flaky. Maybe status should be `PASS`, `WARN`, `FAIL`, `STALE`, `MISSING`.

Allison Grant: From SRE/observability perspective, we can alert on two things:
- New quant_profile appears in telemetry (cardinality guardrail caught it) but no guardrail mapping exists.
- Guardrail status transitions to FAIL and that profile is still receiving >X% of traffic.

Aisha Bello: That second one is easy to wire into alert rules if we have a metric like `requests_by_quant_profile` + `guardrail_status`. We can compute a derived signal in the hourly job.

Aditya Rao: OK but what’s the source of truth for “guardrail status”? Applied ML eval harness? Optimize has prompt sets + regression tests too.

Quentin Harris: I’d strongly prefer: source = eval service output (whatever runs nightly on pinned prompt sets). Optimize can subscribe / reuse.

Marissa Cole: For the dashboard, we just need:
- status label
- last evaluated date
- link to details
We can store a URL string in the view.

Nadia Rahman: We should also tie it to routing: there’s a world where runtime falls back from fp16 to int8 under capacity. If that fallback changes quality, we should still surface it. At minimum: “fallback happened” + quant_profile used.

Aditya Rao: That’s a great point. We can treat fallback profiles as first-class and require guardrails too.

Logan Wright: Practical question: how do we avoid blocking experimentation? We’ll have experimental profiles that we want to try on 1% traffic.

Aditya Rao: Proposal:
- `quantization_profile_tier`: `prod`, `experimental`
- For `experimental`: allow traffic, but show ⚠️ and require explicit allowlist / lower alert severity.
- For `prod`: must have PASS or WARN within 7 days.

Allison Grant: We should align that with feature flags / rollouts. If it’s “experimental” and it gets accidentally routed at scale, that’s an incident.

Quentin Harris: Where does `quantization_profile_tier` live? Model metadata service? Right now model metadata is owned by me; we can add a simple config mapping and publish to both telemetry validation + dashboard.

Nadia Rahman: If we add validation at ingestion, we need a list of allowed quant profiles. That list could include experimental ones but still controlled.

Marissa Cole: Can we define the contract now?
`GET /internal/unit_econ/guardrails?model_id=...&model_version=...`
returns rows keyed by quant profile with fields `status`, `last_run_ts`, `details_url`, `tier`.

Logan Wright: That seems fine. But I’d rather the economics API just reads from warehouse view so we don’t create a new service dependency.

Aditya Rao: +1. Warehouse view is fine for v1.

Aisha Bello: Action item: I’ll draft alert logic once we know the status enums + thresholds.

Quentin Harris: I’ll open a doc / PR for the mapping file + allowed values. We should pin naming to the same strings used in `ENG-8425` (no more “int8v2” vs “w8a8_v2” nonsense).

Nadia Rahman: Please also include a “default unknown” behavior:
- If quant_profile missing, set `quantization_profile='unknown'` (not null)
- That will keep aggregates stable and also make “unknown traffic” visible.

Marissa Cole: For UI we can add a tooltip: “Quality badge reflects last eval run on Redwood prompt set (see link). Does not guarantee customer-specific quality.”

Aditya Rao: Great. Next steps:
- Quentin: propose canonical quant_profile strings + tier + mapping location
- Nadia/Logan: confirm we can plumb a `guardrail_status` dimension into the hourly unit econ aggregates without killing performance
- Marissa: add badge + filter in UI (“Show only FAIL/STALE”)
- Allison/Aisha: alert proposal

Allison Grant: :thumbsup: also please make sure the alert includes runbook link + “who to page” (eng-ml vs runtime vs telemetry). We’ve been burned by ambiguous ownership.

Logan Wright: I’ll sync with Caleb (data quality) too. If we add this as a dimension, it becomes another completeness surface (e.g., % rows with missing guardrail mapping). We should define a threshold.

Aditya Rao: Perfect. Let’s treat “guardrail coverage” as a first-class data quality metric: % of output tokens attributed to quant profiles with PASS/WARN within 7d.

Quentin Harris: Love it. I’ll drop a short spec in the eng-ml channel later today and tag folks for review.

source.content:
Jonas Weber: trying to land a decision on eval sampling rates for route experiments (quality signal stream in Console). biggest concern: when traffic split is small (e.g. 1-5%), we won’t have enough samples + might bias. what should default be?

Olivia Grant: +1. right now the prototype is “sample 1% of requests overall, run prompt-set evals async.” but for 1% variant, that’s like… basically nothing.

Selene Huang: also if we do “first N requests per minute” it’s not random (time-of-day + bursty customers), and if the smaller variant is mostly long-tail tenants we’ll skew.

Logan Wright: from telemetry side: we can do deterministic sampling off request_id/span_id. e.g. `hash(experiment_id, variant_id, request_id)` < threshold. that at least keeps it unbiased vs latency/streaming/etc.

Caleb Johnson: careful with tagging: if you hash on `variant_id` you’ll get independent samples per variant (fine), but you lose the nice property of “same request would have been sampled regardless of variant” (counterfactual). not sure we need that though.

Dr. Maya Srinivasan: we don’t have counterfactual anyway (request only goes to one variant). main requirement is: sample is representative of what that variant saw. deterministic hash on request_id is good.

Marissa Cole: product angle: we need a default that makes the “Quality” card feel alive in the results view during beta. if it’s blank for 12h on a 90/10 split, customers will complain.

Jonas Weber: ok so two related knobs:
1) sampling strategy (random/deterministic)
2) rate / min samples

Jonas Weber: question: do we want to *oversample* smaller variants (so min N per day) or keep same % and just show “insufficient data” until enough?

Selene Huang: if we oversample, it’s not “bias” per se if the inclusion is independent of content. BUT it does change cost and makes guardrail triggers more sensitive for small variants (which might be good). issue is confusion: customers might assume evals are proportional to traffic.

Olivia Grant: also eval harness capacity isn’t infinite. if a customer runs 10 experiments and each tiny variant gets a fixed floor, we can accidentally spend more on evals than serving :grimacing:

Logan Wright: +1. we need caps.

Caleb Johnson: another pitfall: small-variant traffic might be correlated with sticky key choice (customer_id). if customers have a few big tenants, and the 10% bucket happens to catch 1 big tenant, then “representative sample” = mostly that tenant. that’s reality, but it’ll look like “biased” if you’re expecting even mix.

Dr. Maya Srinivasan: that’s why we should position this as “quality monitoring,” not “stat sig.” and we should add UI text: quality score is sampled; may be noisy at low traffic.

Jonas Weber: proposing:
- deterministic hash sampling per variant
- default 0.5% of requests per variant (post-routing)
- minimum 50 scored requests per variant per day (soft floor)
- cap 500 scored requests per variant per day
- guardrail eval only after min 30 samples in window
thoughts?

Marissa Cole: 50/day feels low for customers doing 1-2h tests. can we do something like min 30 per hour for first 6 hours? (and then taper)

Olivia Grant: we can’t guarantee 30/hr if traffic is tiny. but we can guarantee “attempt to sample up to X/hr” with a cap.

Selene Huang: i’d rather phrase as “target rate” + “UI won’t compute deltas until N>=K.” no magic.

Logan Wright: implementation wise, “soft floor” is hard in pure deterministic sampling. we’d need a per-variant token bucket / quota that says “keep sampling until you hit X per hour.” otherwise deterministic % could be 0 for a while.

Caleb Johnson: token bucket sampling is doable but adds state + more failure modes. deterministic hash is stateless and very cheap.

Olivia Grant: what about hybrid:
- default deterministic hash at p=0.5%
- if variant traffic < threshold (say < 200 req/hr), bump p to 2% (still deterministic) so you at least get some.
- also hard cap by absolute count/day.

Jonas Weber: that might be simplest. but does “bump p based on observed traffic” create bias?

Dr. Maya Srinivasan: not bias if the selection is still independent of content and we aren’t selecting based on outcomes. you’re just changing inclusion probability. comparisons across variants remain valid as long as each variant’s sample is random from its own served traffic. the only thing that changes is variance/cost.

Selene Huang: agree. the bigger risk is *perceived* bias / “why does variant B have more evals than variant A when it has less traffic.” we can explain “we increased sampling to reach minimum viable signal.”

Marissa Cole: ok but we should keep it very bounded and visible in Console (maybe tooltip: “sampling rate adjusted due to low traffic”). otherwise support tickets.

Logan Wright: +1. also please don’t make this too dynamic. if we keep changing p every 5m, dashboards will be a mess. pick coarse buckets.

Caleb Johnson: suggest coarse policy:
- if variant allocation >= 20%: p=0.25%
- 5-20%: p=0.5%
- 1-5%: p=1%
- <1%: p=2%
all deterministic by `hash(request_id, experiment_id, variant_id)`
with cap 500/day/variant

Olivia Grant: those numbers seem reasonable. but we should sanity check against eval cost. do we have rough RPS assumptions for beta?

Logan Wright: for beta, most experiments are on a couple routes with ~10-200 rps. even at 2% that’s 0.2-4 rps worst case. with cap 500/day we’re safe.

Selene Huang: guardrails: we should require a min sample size before emitting “quality regression” alert. otherwise one bad prompt triggers alert. i propose:
- don’t evaluate quality guardrail until N>=50 in last 6h (or last 24h for low traffic)
- use rolling median or trimmed mean, not mean

Dr. Maya Srinivasan: +1 on robust estimator. median is easiest to explain.

Jonas Weber: ok decision shaping up:
1) sampling is deterministic hash, post-routing, per variant
2) p varies by allocation bucket (coarse)
3) hard cap per variant per day
4) quality regression guardrail only after min N; use median

Marissa Cole: can we also agree on what to do when split is super small and we don’t hit N? Console should show “insufficient data” not “0.00”.

Olivia Grant: yep. we’ll return null/empty + message.

Caleb Johnson: one more note: for streaming requests + retries, ensure request_id is stable. if request_id changes on retry, we’ll double-sample weirdly.

Logan Wright: we fixed attribution on streaming retries last week, but sampling should probably use the same stable key we use for assignment context (`rw_request_id`). i’ll sync with Vikram/Amara.

Jonas Weber: cool. i’ll write this up as the proposed default in the eval integration doc.

Dr. Maya Srinivasan: please include explicit non-goal: this isn’t guaranteeing statistical power. and add a warning that very low allocation variants will have slow-moving quality metrics.

Marissa Cole: +1. also add a line for support: “if you want faster quality signal, temporarily increase traffic to variant.”

Jonas Weber: summary (proposal, unless objections by EOD):
- Deterministic sampling key: `hash(experiment_id, variant_id, rw_request_id)`
- Sampling rate p by allocation (based on configured split, not observed traffic):
  - >=20%: 0.25%
  - 5-20%: 0.5%
  - 1-5%: 1%
  - <1%: 2%
- Cap: max 500 eval-scored requests / variant / day
- Console: show “Insufficient data” until min N met
- Guardrail computation: use rolling median; require N>=50 in window before alerting

Selene Huang: works for me :thumbsup:

Logan Wright: :thumbsup: and i’ll make sure we expose “effective sampling rate” as a field so Console can tooltip it.

Olivia Grant: :thumbsup: i’ll update the harness config defaults + note the cap.

Caleb Johnson: :thumbsup: pls ping me when you wire the stable request id in sampling.

Marissa Cole: :thumbsup: thanks all. will update beta enablement FAQ accordingly.

source.messages:
Marissa Cole: hey folks — need to lock down what “quality” means in Console for route experiments. We keep saying “quality metric” but it’s fuzzy. For beta we need something we can defend + explain in UI. Can we align today on: (1) what eval score(s) we surface, (2) normalization (so it’s not random 0.73 vs 0.81), (3) drift / when to invalidate.

Jonas Weber: +1. From eval harness POV we can emit a few different things:
- per-prompt scores (raw)
- per-suite aggregate (weighted avg)
- task breakdown (format adherence, correctness proxy, safety, etc)
Question is what becomes the single number in Console.

Selene Huang: also pls define if the number is absolute or “delta vs control”. If it’s absolute, customers will assume it’s comparable across routes/models which it won’t be.

Olivia Grant: for integration we already have a planned stream like:
`{route_id, experiment_id, variant_id, eval_suite_id, score_name, raw_score, ts}`
We can also add `normalized_score` but need to know how we normalize.

Dr. Maya Srinivasan: constraints from Applied ML:
- Quality must be *interpret-able* and stable across time.
- Avoid “one metric to rule them all” that gets gamed.
- For initial release, I’d rather show a *small set* of canonical scores than a custom free-for-all.

Alicia Perez: UI question: are we thinking a single “Quality” card (green/red) + optional breakdown drawer? We can do tooltips + “based on Redwood eval suite” copy, but we need crisp semantics.

Logan Wright: telemetry note: whatever we pick, please avoid exploding cardinality (score_name x suite x route x variant x region). We can handle a couple of fields, but not a dozen per request. If it’s asynchronous evals (not per request), easier.

Marissa Cole: yep — this is *not* per-request. It’s optional eval pipeline attribution per variant, and Console shows it alongside latency/cost/error. But we need a default “quality” definition.

Jonas Weber: proposal for beta:
- Default eval suite: `route_experiments_v1`
- Suite produces 0..1 for each prompt with a rubric; aggregate is weighted mean
- Console shows:
  1) “Quality (eval)” = aggregate suite score (0..100)
  2) “Δ vs control” = variant_score - control_score (in points)
This keeps absolute + delta.

Selene Huang: I’m strongly against showing the absolute as 0..100 without a big caveat. People will compare across routes, then complain it’s inconsistent.

Dr. Maya Srinivasan: agree. Absolute is fine only if it’s clearly “within this prompt set + eval suite” and not an overall product KPI.

Alicia Perez: we can label it “Eval score (route prompt set)” and default the main UI to delta. Absolute could be in the breakdown panel.

Marissa Cole: delta-first makes sense. But customers also ask “is variant B better” and a delta needs context if control is 12 vs 92.

Jonas Weber: we can show both but make delta prominent.

Logan Wright: if we show both, we need to ensure both are computed on the same sampling window + filters. Otherwise support tickets.

Olivia Grant: re: normalization — raw scores from different rubrics aren’t comparable. Even within a suite, scores can drift as we update prompts or judge model. We need a stable mapping.

Selene Huang: on drift: prompt set changes should invalidate history. Also if we update the judge model / rubric version, we should reset or version the metric.

Jonas Weber: +1. We can bake versioning into eval suite id, e.g. `route_experiments_v1` and bump to v2 when rubric/judge changes.

Dr. Maya Srinivasan: normalization suggestion (simple, defensible):
- For each eval suite version + prompt set id, compute score distribution for the *control* variant during first N hours/days.
- Report variant deltas in “points” and optionally z-score relative to control distribution.
- If control distribution drifts beyond threshold (e.g., prompt set edits), flag “baseline changed” and stop showing trend.
No need for fancy stat-sig.

Marissa Cole: do we have “prompt set id” today as a real concept in product, or is it internal?

Jonas Weber: internal today, but we can surface it as “Eval configuration” in the UI (read-only). For beta, it may just be “Redwood default prompt set” vs “Customer-provided prompt set” (if we allow attach).

Olivia Grant: for Phase 2 we planned “attach eval suite” in Console. I’d rather treat prompt set as explicit object with id + version.

Dylan Brooks: jumping in — from Console standpoint, we can show:
- Quality: “Not configured” state unless user attaches evals
- If configured: show delta prominently; absolute available in tooltip
But PM wants defaults to “just work” for beta design partners.

Marissa Cole: yes, beta ask is “I swap model variants; tell me if outputs got worse.” So default eval suite + prompt set must exist.

Selene Huang: default prompt set can’t be generic across customers. It needs to match the route use case or it’s misleading.

Dr. Maya Srinivasan: there’s a middle ground: default “sanity suite” that checks generic regressions:
- refusal / policy compliance (don’t go off the rails)
- formatting / JSON validity (if structured output route)
- extreme toxicity
But that’s not “quality” in the helpfulness sense.

Jonas Weber: we can scope default to: “format adherence + safety + hallucination proxy” and call it “Output quality (sanity checks)”. That’s closer to what we can defend broadly.

Alicia Perez: naming matters a lot. If we call it “Quality” people assume it means “better answers.” If it’s safety/format, it should be labeled.

Marissa Cole: ok, so two options:
A) Keep label “Quality” but define it as “Eval score for your attached prompt set” (requires configuration)
B) Provide default “Sanity score” always on (route-agnostic) and call it that
We can do both? (sanity always, quality optional)

Logan Wright: two metrics is fine if they’re async aggregates. Please keep to a small set of score_names: `sanity_score`, `task_score` (optional) and maybe `format_score` if needed.

Olivia Grant: +1. In eval output we can emit:
- `sanity_score` (0..1)
- `quality_score` (0..1) only when prompt set attached
- `n_samples`
- `suite_version`
That’s manageable.

Selene Huang: and we should *never* average scores across different prompt sets. If prompt set changes, show “insufficient data” until we have enough samples again.

Jonas Weber: sampling: we need to avoid bias when split is small. I propose:
- nightly job per experiment
- target 200 prompts per variant per run (cap)
- minimum 30 prompts before showing anything
- if a variant has low traffic, we sample from recent traffic logs with deterministic seed to avoid flapping

Dr. Maya Srinivasan: careful: sampling from traffic logs can introduce privacy / PII issues depending on customer data. For now, do we have a safe “prompt capture” pipeline? Otherwise we should require customer-provided prompt set for “quality_score”.

Marissa Cole: good point. For beta we *do* have 2 design partners willing to provide prompt sets. Let’s not promise auto-capture in GA.

Dylan Brooks: that fits the PRD: “optional integration to attach eval scores (prompt set quality signals)”. So default “quality_score” only exists if attached.

Alicia Perez: UI plan then:
- Card 1: “Sanity (eval)” always available (if we can run generic checks without customer prompts??)
- Card 2: “Quality (eval)” only when eval config attached
But if we can’t run sanity without prompts, both become “attach prompt set” anyway.

Jonas Weber: sanity still needs prompts. Unless it’s run on synthetic prompts, which isn’t representative. I’d rather not show it unless attached.

Selene Huang: +1. Synthetic prompts = false confidence.

Marissa Cole: ok: in beta + GA, “quality” metrics appear only if user attaches eval configuration (prompt set + suite). Otherwise Console shows “No quality data configured” with CTA.

Olivia Grant: then normalization: can we agree on:
- store raw 0..1
- display as 0..100 “points”
- main display is Δ points vs control
- also show absolute points in tooltip

Selene Huang: yes, but require we also show `n_samples` and a “data freshness” timestamp. Otherwise we get “quality not updating” tickets.

Logan Wright: +100. Also we should emit `eval_run_id` so support can correlate.

Dr. Maya Srinivasan: drift rules / invalidation:
- prompt set has version; if version changes, reset the time series (new baseline)
- eval suite version changes => reset baseline
- if control changes model_id mid-experiment (shouldn’t, but can happen), flag and stop computing deltas until stable

Jonas Weber: we can enforce “control variant immutable while experiment running” at config validation layer. If model changes, require stop + new experiment.

Marissa Cole: that’s aligned with rollouts constraints, but there are cases where people update a pinned model version. We should at least detect and banner.

Dylan Brooks: banner copy idea: “Quality comparisons paused: control variant changed (model/version). Create a new experiment to resume.”

Alicia Perez: works.

Selene Huang: what about multi-metric suites? e.g. helpfulness + correctness + style. Do we collapse into one number?

Dr. Maya Srinivasan: for now, yes, one “quality_score” per suite. But include breakdown in JSON (internal) for future UI.

Olivia Grant: we can include breakdown as extra fields in the eval artifact, but Console API can initially only expose aggregate to avoid more UI work.

Logan Wright: + keep the storage flexible but the surfaced metrics minimal.

Marissa Cole: summarizing what I’m hearing (tell me if wrong):
1) “Quality” in Console = eval suite aggregate score attached to experiment variants; not computed unless user attaches eval config.
2) Display delta vs control as primary; absolute score secondary.
3) Score shown as 0..100 points derived from raw 0..1.
4) Versioning: prompt set version + eval suite version are part of identity; if either changes, reset baseline and show “insufficient data”.
5) Show sample size + last run timestamp; if control changes, pause comparisons + banner.

Jonas Weber: yes. Also we should define default suite when attaching: `route_experiments_quality_v1` (name bikeshed) with documented rubric.

Selene Huang: +1, and we should avoid the term “statistically significant” anywhere. Just “directional” + guardrails.

Alicia Perez: UX action item for me: update label from “Quality” to “Eval score” in the first iteration, with tooltip explaining it’s specific to the attached prompt set + suite. If PM insists on “Quality”, we need a stronger tooltip.

Marissa Cole: I’m ok with “Eval score” as the label. “Quality” can be the section header maybe.

Olivia Grant: engineering action item: I’ll update the integration doc to include these required fields:
- `eval_suite_id`, `eval_suite_version`
- `prompt_set_id`, `prompt_set_version`
- `score_name=quality_score`
- `raw_score`, `score_points`
- `n_samples`, `eval_run_ts`
And add “reset baseline on version change” behavior.

Logan Wright: I’ll sync w/ Caleb/Mei on the dataset schema so we don’t break rollups. Also will confirm retention — eval runs are lower volume but we should still cap.

Dr. Maya Srinivasan: I’ll draft a short definition for docs: “Eval score measures performance on your attached prompt set using Redwood’s evaluation suite. It’s best-effort directional signal, not a guarantee.”

Marissa Cole: perfect. I’ll paste this into PRD + ping Console folks. thanks all :thumbsup:

Selene Huang: :thumbsup: pls also add a note: “Do not compare scores across different routes or prompt sets.”

Alicia Perez: adding to tooltip + empty state.

Jonas Weber: I’ll also add a “minimum samples” recommendation (>=30) to avoid noisy deltas. Might be a hard threshold in API.

Dylan Brooks: if we hard threshold, make sure the results API returns `insufficient_data=true` so UI can handle gracefully.

Olivia Grant: yep, will include. end.

- per-prompt scores (raw)
- per-suite aggregate (weighted avg)
- task breakdown (format adherence, correctness proxy, safety, etc)

5) Show sample size + last run timestamp; if control changes, pause comparisons + banner.

Jonas Weber: yes. Also we should define default suite when attaching: `route_experiments_quality_v1` (name bikeshed) with documented rubric.

Selene Huang: +1, and we should avoid the term “statistically significant” anywhere. Just “directional” + guardrails.

Alicia Perez: UX action item for me: update label from “Quality” to “Eval score” in the first iteration, with tooltip explaining it’s specific to the attached prompt set + suite. If PM insists on “Quality”, we need a stronger tooltip.

Marissa Cole: I’m ok with “Eval score” as the label. “Quality” can be the section header maybe.

Olivia Grant: engineering action item: I’ll update the integration doc to include these required fields:
- `eval_suite_id`, `eval_suite_version`
- `prompt_set_id`, `prompt_set_version`
- `score_name=quality_score`
- `raw_score`, `score_points`
- `n_samples`, `eval_run_ts`
And add “reset baseline on version change” behavior.

Logan Wright: I’ll sync w/ Caleb/Mei on the dataset schema so we don’t break rollups. Also will confirm retention — eval runs are lower volume but we should still cap.

Dr. Maya Srinivasan: I’ll draft a short definition for docs: “Eval score measures performance on your attached prompt set using Redwood’s evaluation suite. It’s best-effort directional signal, not a guarantee.”

Marissa Cole: perfect. I’ll paste this into PRD + ping Console folks. thanks all :thumbsup:

Selene Huang: :thumbsup: pls also add a note: “Do not compare scores across different routes or prompt sets.”

Alicia Perez: adding to tooltip + empty state.

Jonas Weber: I’ll also add a “minimum samples” recommendation (>=30) to avoid noisy deltas. Might be a hard threshold in API.

Dylan Brooks: if we hard threshold, make sure the results API returns `insufficient_data=true` so UI can handle gracefully.

Olivia Grant: yep, will include. end.

source.text:
Sara Li: Quick snapshot from last night's micro-bringup for nova-lite-9b. Ran int6/int8 and a hybrid-fallback variant. TL;DR: int6 cuts cost ~42% vs fp16 but we saw a small hit on long-form summarization recall (esp >800 tokens).

Dan Cho: can you paste the perf/cost numbers?

Sara Li: Yep — small table below (rough averages from 10k sample). Note latency is p95 on our test harness.

Sara Li: ```
model                     | cost/unit | latency p95 | long-recall @800+
nova-lite-9b-fp16         | $1.00     | 210ms       | 0.82
nova-lite-9b-int8         | $0.62     | 230ms       | 0.80
nova-lite-9b-int6         | $0.58     | 260ms       | 0.76
hybrid(int6->fp16 fallback)| $0.63     | 215ms avg    | 0.80
```

Maya R.: That p95 jump on int6 is concerning for chat flows. where's the latency coming from? kv-cache misses?

Mateo Alvarez: int6 currently forces a slightly different kernel path that doesn't use optimized fused attention for seq>512 — we hit a slower fallback for long sequences. Also cache alignment for our batching is less effective (more padding).

Kevin Patel: Product (Priya) — for GTN, do we target short QA/snippets or long summaries? That changes the recommendation.

Priya Singh: GTN will run mixed workloads but heavy on multi-turn conversation and occasional long-form summarization (~15% of requests >700 tokens). Latency SLO is 250ms p95. Cost sensitivity moderate.

Dan Cho: Given that, pure int6 misses the latency SLO and hurts recall. hybrid looks attractive — cost ~37% savings vs fp16 and p95 ~215ms which meets SLO.

Sara Li: Agree. Also ran a quick quality regression check vs our baseline prompt set. int6 had a 4.5% drop in Rouge-L on long docs, int8 1.5% drop, hybrid ~1.6% drop (because fallback triggers on long contexts).

Priya Singh: Product priority: keep quality >0.80 recall and hit latency SLO. Hybrid seems acceptable. Any operational caveats?

Mateo Alvarez: Implementation notes: need routing flag + fast route to fp16 for contexts with >512 tokens. That requires a lightweight context-length classifier at router (cheap). Also need to tune padding/batch heuristics to avoid p95 inflation.

Kevin Patel: Risk: fallback increases cross-model traffic which complicates cost accounting and cache warmstart. But unit econ still better than fp16.

Sara Li: Suggested rollout plan: 1) Canary hybrid in us-east on 10% traffic; 2) collect quality and tail latency slices; 3) if long-doc recall stays within -1.5% of baseline, expand to 50%; 4) enable autoscale adjustments for bursty sessions.

Dan Cho: sounds good. Can we add a small eval hook that raises alert if fallback rate >12% or p95 >250ms?

Mateo Alvarez: yep, I'll add tracing + metric (fallback_rate, p95_by_seq_bucket). ops-bot: create rollout ticket?

ops-bot: Created ticket RW-1428: "Hybrid nova-lite-9b bringup (canary + metrics)" assigned to Mateo, Sara, Dan.

Priya Singh: Approved from product. Please include a one-pager for GTN explaining UX expectations if rare quality regressions happen (graceful retry to fp16).

Sara Li: Will do. I'll post the canary checklist and a short snippet for the router rule later today.

Kevin Patel: Final recommendation for GTN: roll hybrid int6->fp16 with conservative fallback threshold and monitor long-doc quality. Approve? :thumbsup:

Priya Singh: :thumbsup:

Maya R.: :thumbsup:

Dan Cho: let's set a two-week canary window and reconvene with slice stats.

Sara Li: Thanks all — launching canary today EOD. I'll drop links to dashboards when live.

Mateo Alvarez: I'll instrument the metrics and alert thresholds.

Priya Singh: Appreciate the fast turnaround.

Sara Li: Link to supporting eval notebook: https://redwood.internal/notes/nova-lite-9b-eval (internal)

source.text:
Ava Chen: Heads-up — we're planning a 5% staggered rollout of model v5.1-int4-nf4 (bridge variant) tomorrow 10:00 PT. Want to lock the stability gates and auto-fallback thresholds before we flip.
Jordan Patel: Slices we care about? priority customers + longform doc summarization, right?
Ava Chen: Yep. priority customers (acct tier: enterprise), longform (>1024 tokens), and short-chat. Also a generic random sample slice for baseline.
Mike Torres: Quick summary from today's staging shadow run: overall MRR +0.6%, p95 latency +12ms, error rate flat, hallucination rate +0.9 percentage points on longform slice. Small sample though (~6k requests).
Sophia Li: Thanks Mike. That hallucination bump on longform is the main concern. We should set an auto-fallback if hallucination increases by >1.0pp or if MRR drops by >1.5%. Also consider p95 threshold at +30ms absolute.
Jordan Patel: Agree. Need a clear sampling and decision window: propose evaluate every 10k live requests per slice OR every 15 minutes (whichever comes first) during the 5% ramp.
Mike Torres: Also add a quiet-burn guard: if metrics oscillate (sign change) 3 times in a row within the window, pause automated promotion and notify #eng-oncall.
Ava Chen: Sounds good. Can we codify into the rollout policy? Minimal config:
```yaml
rollout:
  phases:
    - pct: 5
      duration: 2h
  evaluation:
    slices: [priority-customers, longform, short-chat, baseline]
    sample_window: 10000
    max_time: 15m
    metrics:
      mrr:
        threshold_down: -1.5 # percent
      hallucination_rate:
        threshold_up: 1.0 # percentage points
      p95_latency_ms:
        threshold_up: 30 # ms
    oscillation_guard: 3 # toggles
    on_failure: auto-fallback
```
Jordan Patel: Minor tweak: make the hallucination threshold 0.9pp for longform slice specifically (more sensitive). For other slices keep 1.0pp.
Sophia Li: Also require that auto-fallback only triggers if the change is statistically significant at p<0.05 or after at least 5k examples in that slice — prevents flip on tiny noise.
Mike Torres: Good call. We'll use bootstrap CI on the difference and require n>=5k per slice. eval-bot will post the CI every window.
Ava Chen: Can someone post the final rollout snippet to the config repo once we agree? I'll schedule the 10:00 PT start and tag infra. @deploy-bot can you do the scheduled deploy and post a follow-up? :thumbsup:
deploy-bot: Scheduled: v5.1-int4-nf4 bridge -> 5% at 2026-03-21T10:00:00-07:00. Will run evaluations per policy and post updates to this thread.
eval-bot: First window (baseline) on shadow run: n=6200, MRR delta +0.6% (95% CI [+0.1, +1.1]), hallucination delta +0.9pp (95% CI [+0.4, +1.4]). Longform slice n=1.8k in shadow -> not enough for auto decision (need 5k).
Jordan Patel: OK — longform needs more burn-in. Keep short-chat and baseline under watch. If longform hits 5k and shows >0.9pp hallucination increase with p<0.05 we rollback.
Sophia Li: I'll add an alert to the console dashboard: threshold crossing + CI check -> auto-fallback action. Also add human-in-loop notify for priority customers.
Ava Chen: Nice. One last item — logging: include sample prompts + model outputs for any hallucination-exceeding examples (scrub PII) and attach to the incident ticket automatically.
Mike Torres: I'll add the logging hook in the inference runtime.
Ava Chen: Great — let's follow up after the first 2-hour phase. Thanks all. :rocket:

source.text:
Aisha Khan: quick follow-up on the precision explainability RFC that landed this morning — we still need to decide ownership and the deprecation/playbook for old quant profiles. main reqs i think: per-response explainability fields (quality_delta estimate, profile_id, fallback_reason), stable header for client compatibility, and a migration cadence. who wants to own the playbook?

Ben Liu: do we expect the runtime to compute the `quality_delta` at inference time or should that be produced offline by eval and attached via profile metadata?

Clara Martinez: my leaning is runtime-provided, but only an estimate. we can use cached calibration maps produced offline + a small runtime heuristic. example config sketch: 

```
precision_profile:
  id: "fp8-lite-v2"
  explain_mode: runtime
  explain_fields: ["quality_delta_est","fallback_reason","profile_id"]
  expose_headers: ["X-Quant-Profile","X-Quality-Est"]
```

Clara Martinez: runtime estimate keeps clients simple (single response) and lets ops emit per-request telemetry. offline eval still calibrates the maps used for the estimate.

Dan O'Neill: +1 but watch backward compat. older clients will expect the old JSON schema. propose we always include `explain` field as nullable object and keep top-level tokens same. also add a stable response header `X-Quant-Profile: fp8-lite-v2` so proxies and SRE can route.

Ethan Park: for observability i want a single histogram metric `redwood.quant.quality_delta` tagged by `profile_id`, `model`, `customer_id` (where allowed). alert: quality-regression if p95 > threshold for 24h. also we should surface a TTL'd daily rollout delta for new profiles.

Clara Martinez: privacy note — scrub prompt/response PII before attaching any per-request explainability blob to telemetry. probably do hash-only customer ids unless the tenant opted-in.

Aisha Khan: agreed on privacy. deprecation cadence proposal: 90-day notice + 30-day soft-fallback window where runtime serves a compatibility shim that adds `explain` = null + header, and telemetry still records legacy metrics. after 120 days auto-redirect to next-best profile and mark former profile deprecated in catalog UI. we should make rollback easy for first 2 weeks.

Ben Liu: i can own the playbook draft. action items: 1) draft playbook (who, cadence, rollback), 2) runtime impl spec for `quality_delta_est`, 3) telemetry labels and alert thresholds, 4) API compatibility contract. i'll ping folks in this thread when draft ready.

deploy-bot: deploy succeeded for branch `quant-explainability/shim` — id 1719509999.1 :white_check_mark:

Aisha Khan: awesome, thanks @Ben Liu. i'll add product + legal to review the privacy bits and then we can schedule a short canary next week. action: Ben to create PR for playbook, Clara to open runtime spec PR, Ethan to propose alert thresholds.

Ben Liu: filing PR today, thanks all :rocket:
source._post_process_note: this thread is a concise RFC follow-up: ownership and operationalization for explainability + deprecation for quant precision profiles. contains config sketch, telemetry/compat recommendations, and action items.

Aisha Khan: agreed on privacy. deprecation cadence proposal: 90-day notice + 30-day soft-fallback window where runtime serves a compatibility shim that adds `explain` = null + header, and telemetry still records legacy metrics. after 120 days auto-redirect to next-best profile and mark former profile deprecated in catalog UI. we should make rollback easy for first 2 weeks.

Ben Liu: i can own the playbook draft. action items: 1) draft playbook (who, cadence, rollback), 2) runtime impl spec for `quality_delta_est`, 3) telemetry labels and alert thresholds, 4) API compatibility contract. i'll ping folks in this thread when draft ready.

text:
Aisha Khan: quick follow-up on the precision explainability RFC that landed this morning — we still need to decide ownership and the deprecation/playbook for old quant profiles. main reqs i think: per-response explainability fields (quality_delta estimate, profile_id, fallback_reason), stable header for client compatibility, and a migration cadence. who wants to own the playbook?

Ben Liu: do we expect the runtime to compute the `quality_delta` at inference time or should that be produced offline by eval and attached via profile metadata?

Clara Martinez: my leaning is runtime-provided, but only an estimate. we can use cached calibration maps produced offline + a small runtime heuristic. example config sketch: 

```
precision_profile:
  id: "fp8-lite-v2"
  explain_mode: runtime
  explain_fields: ["quality_delta_est","fallback_reason","profile_id"]
  expose_headers: ["X-Quant-Profile","X-Quality-Est"]
```

Clara Martinez: runtime estimate keeps clients simple (single response) and lets ops emit per-request telemetry. offline eval still calibrates the maps used for the estimate.

Dan O'Neill: +1 but watch backward compat. older clients will expect the old JSON schema. propose we always include `explain` field as nullable object and keep top-level tokens same. also add a stable response header `X-Quant-Profile: fp8-lite-v2` so proxies and SRE can route.

Ethan Park: for observability i want a single histogram metric `redwood.quant.quality_delta` tagged by `profile_id`, `model`, `customer_id` (where allowed). alert: quality-regression if p95 > threshold for 24h. also we should surface a TTL'd daily rollout delta for new profiles.

source.messages:
Marta Liu: Quick sanity check — onboarding llamaX-7b-int8-64k shows a context-window handoff issue when resuming from a saved cache around token ~48000. Cold run matches eval, resumed run diverges after ~3 tokens.
Diego Ramos: Can you repro deterministically? sounds like an index drift in the resume path.
Marta Liu: Yep, deterministic seed 42. Repro steps below, very small script. Cold vs resumed outputs differ starting right after resume.
Marta Liu: ```
# repro (run on staging with model build 2026.03.10-rc3)
python tools/gen.py --model llamaX-7b-int8 --max-tokens 64000 --seed 42 --snapshot out/snap.bin --save-snapshot
python tools/gen.py --model llamaX-7b-int8 --resume out/snap.bin --seed 42 --max-tokens 64005 --out out/resumed.json
python tools/gen.py --model llamaX-7b-int8 --max-tokens 64005 --seed 42 --out out/cold.json
python tools/compare.py out/cold.json out/resumed.json --metric kl --dump-first 10
```
Ava Chen: Thanks — which shard/mapping is this hitting? we did the mmap refactor last week, could be a copy/alignment issue.
Diego Ramos: I ran your script locally on one shard: KL after resume ~0.018 and top1 token mismatch at step +3 consistently. Looks like a one-row offset in the cached key indices after a multi-page mmap+cudaMemcpy.
Sora Patel: Which build? if it's the refactor branch I touched, I can check flags.
Marta Liu: Build tag 2026.03.10-rc3. Also tried quant modes: int8 and q4_0. Curiously int8 reproduced with slightly smaller KL than q4_0.
Diego Ramos: Huh — quant rounding reducing the observed delta is plausible. Also check attention mask alignment on resume: an off-by-one in mask application would produce small but consistent divergence.
Ava Chen: I can schedule a quick bisect across the mmap refactor commit range. Will run on staging cluster and report.
Marta Liu: Eval numbers: LongStoriesBench perplexity cold=9.8, resumed=12.7 (same seed). So it's visible in downstream metrics, not just raw logits.
Diego Ramos: Not huge but consistent. We should add a defensive checksum per-snapshot-chunk to catch index shifts on load.
Sora Patel: I'll audit mmap flags + page alignment + cudaMemcpy offsets tonight. If a page is misaligned we can shift the first KV row by bytes.
ci-bot: Build 2026.03.10-rc3 scheduled: bisect job queued (staging, 5 seeds, 3 quant modes).
Marta Liu: For quick debugging, here are the first 6 logits (softmax) for cold vs resumed at divergence point: cold [0.12,0.08,0.07,0.05,0.03,0.02] resumed [0.11,0.09,0.06,0.06,0.03,0.02] — tiny but repeatable.
Diego Ramos: :thinking_face: ok small drift but deterministic. I'll try adding an assert that compares per-row checksums during resume and log offsets.
Ava Chen: If the assert flags, we can fail fast and capture the raw snapshot bytes for forensic. I'll prepare a quick patch to run on the bisect.
Sora Patel: Plan: 1) reproduce on 3 seeds 2) run bisect across mmap refactor commits 3) add per-chunk CRC guard and re-run 4) run smoke across dedicated cluster if fixed.
Marta Liu: Sounds good. I'll run the initial multi-seed batch now and drop the logs here. Thanks all.
Diego Ramos: Thx — ping if you want me to take the bisect runs.
Ava Chen: I'll coordinate infra runs and collect artifacts into /staging/artifacts/context-handoff/1718801234.
Marta Liu: will update with artifacts and a short summary after runs finish.

source.messages:
Priya Sinha: hey folks — seeing a weird CI pattern: when contributors push a quick follow-up commit the GH Actions -> Buildkite bridge sometimes spawns two eval runs that both upload artifacts named the same. one of the runners then errors trying to download the artifact (collision). anyone seen this?
Ben Carter: yep saw that last week. looks like webhook debounce + retry creates 2 workflow runs with different run_ids but same artifact path.
Sam Nguyen: we added a debounce in the GH side but Buildkite triggers can still be retried by the actions runner. retries + auto-retry for failed uploads = duplicate artifacts.
Priya Sinha: right — failure mode is: older job finishes upload later and overwrites the artifact while newer job already started pulling -> checksum mismatch and eval fails. not great for PR gating.
Luis Rodriguez: quick mitigation: include the workflow_run.id or run_id in the artifact name. e.g. `artifact-${{ github.run_id }}.tar.gz`. that avoids collisions but doesn't stop duplicate compute.
Anna Patel: also we should cancel earlier builds on new push. GitHub has `concurrency: group: ${{ github.ref }}` to cancel in-flight workflows. can we do that in our eval workflows?
Sam Nguyen: we tried `concurrency` but because the GH action merely enqueues a Buildkite job via API, the cancel doesn't kill the already-created buildkite build. need to wire cancellation to Buildkite too (API call).
Ben Carter: idea: GH action uses `concurrency` so only one workflow run is active, and in the `on: workflow_run` or post-step we call Buildkite API to cancel any builds with the same branch + older commit SHA.
Priya Sinha: sounds promising. but still worried about the race where both builds run long enough that both upload. can we make artifact upload idempotent?
Luis Rodriguez: we can have the runner upload to a `temp/<run_id>/` path and then do an atomic rename after successful checksum. rename will fail if destination exists — then uploader can backoff and abort. example: ```bash
TMP=artifacts/temp/$RUN_ID.tar.gz
FINAL=artifacts/$PR_NUMBER-latest.tar.gz
upload $TMP && if mv $TMP $FINAL; then echo ok; else echo already exists; fi
```
Anna Patel: +1 to atomic rename. Also we should surface which run is the authoritative one in PR checks. prefer showing the latest-run check as required.
Priya Sinha: on gating: can we use the Checks API to mark older evals as "skipped" and only count the latest? that way branch protection only considers latest evaluation.
Ben Carter: yes — we can post a check_run with `conclusion: neutral` for older ones and `conclusion: success/failure` for latest. need to ensure the integration has permissions and we don't hit rate limits.
Sam Nguyen: Buildkite also has an API to cancel builds; I'll add a step in the GH action that triggers cancel for any builds with `commit < older sha >` on same branch before creating new one.
Luis Rodriguez: volunteering to prototype the atomic upload + rename in the runner. will add a small helper in runner utils: `safe_upload(artifact, final_path)` and test with concurrent uploads.
Anna Patel: i'll update the GH workflow template to include `concurrency` and a post-step for Buildkite cancel + add a `GITHUB_RUN_ID` env var into the build metadata so builds are traceable.
ci-bot: automated note — PR #482: eval rerun requested by @maria. buildkite id 8453 canceled. :white_check_mark:
Priya Sinha: awesome. action items: Sam (GH cancel wiring), Luis (atomic upload PR), Anna (workflow update). I'll loop back once we have a prototype — thanks y'all :rocket:
Ben Carter: can we also add a small metric in Datadog for artifact collisions / checksum mismatches so we know if fixes reduce incidents?
Priya Sinha: yes pls — add `eval.artifact.collision` increment on any checksum mismatch. that'll help quantify impact.
Sam Nguyen: noted. I'll add a Datadog event when cancel API runs too.
Luis Rodriguez: starting branch `runner/safe-upload` now. will ping when ready for review.
Priya Sinha: sweet, closing the loop for now. if anyone sees new failures tag me. :pray:

source.messages:
Ravi Singh: Quick sync - we need clearer rollback/hysteresis rules for low-bit rollouts. current 'fail fast' reverts are noisy for transient noise.
Olivia Park: Agreed. My proposal: add a hysteresis window so a single spike doesn't auto-revert. e.g., require 3 consecutive windows (1m windows) breaching threshold before revert.
Mei Tan: +1. Also add a cool-down after a revert to avoid flip-flop (e.g., 30m hold).
Jordan Blake: We should separate metrics that trigger immediate safety revert (safety-critical degradation) vs those that are soft (quality delta). Immediate metric: safety_token_mismatch or hallucination_count above X -> immediate revert. Soft metric: human-eval proxy (degradation_score) -> hysteresis.
Ravi Singh: Good. Concrete thresholds suggestion: safety metric -> immediate. quality metric -> breach if degradation_rate > 0.6% for 3 windows. latency -> breach if p95 delta > 5ms for 3 windows.
Olivia Park: Also support per-tenant sensitivity: enterprise customers might set tighter thresholds, small tenants looser. Add tenant_override in profile meta.
Mei Tan: Need to define windowing semantics. suggest sliding windows with step = 30s, window_size = 60s, requiring N consecutive breached windows. That smooths short spikes while still being responsive.
Jordan Blake: For revert action, plan two modes: auto-revert (default for non-safety issues) and gated-revert (requires human ack for safety_critical models/tenants). Controlled by profile metadata: auto_revert: true/false.
Ravi Singh: Add provenance tagging to the revert: include profile_id, signer, canary_percent, triggered_metric, window_summary. Keep it compact in alert payload for postmortems.
Olivia Park: Telemetry impact: we mustn't explode cardinality. We'll store aggregated counters per-deploy + sampled detailed traces for breached windows. Keep per-tenant summaries only when tenant has overrides.
Mei Tan: Playbook: when auto-revert happens create an incident with severity based on metric. For soft reverts, auto-create a rollback ticket but tag for triage. Ops-bot?
ops-bot: Created ML-4401: "Quant rollback hysteresis + playbook" assigned to Ravi Singh, Olivia Park, Mei Tan, Jordan Blake. https://redwood.atlassian.com/browse/ML-4401 :white_check_mark:
Jordan Blake: We need a lint step that prevents profiles from setting auto_revert=true combined with aggressive thresholds for safety_critical ops. Block if unsafe.
Ravi Singh: Good call. Also add a manual "hold" flag to temporarily disable auto-revert for debugging canaries. Admin-only.
Olivia Park: UI needs to show current holograph of hysteresis state per-deploy (OK / warning (1/3) / breached (3/3) / reverted). That helps SREs see progress.
Mei Tan: For postmortems we want a compact event line: "ts, deploy, profile_id, tenant, metric, windows=[0.2,0.7,0.8], action=revert". That makes search easy.
Jordan Blake: How about a graceful degrade path instead of full revert? e.g., step back from fp8->int8 globally instead of full rollback to model-level. That can buy time while ML investigates.
Ravi Singh: I like staged rollback options: revert_to_previous_signed_profile, fallback_level_one (e.g., move to next entry in fallback chain), or full-disable-operator_level. Profile should define preferred rollback modality.
Olivia Park: Security: ensure revert commands are audited and require 2FA for manual overrides on prod.
Mei Tan: Implementation items: 1) runtime: sliding-window aggregator + hysteresis state machine. 2) telemetry: compact window summaries + sampled traces. 3) ML: mapping from degradation_score -> fallback severity. 4) product: UI states and manual hold button.
Ravi Singh: Timeline? want PoC for hysteresis state machine in two weeks.
Jordan Blake: I'll prototype the state machine and staged rollback hooks this week.
Olivia Park: I'll draft the UI states and incident templates.
Mei Tan: I'll implement telemetry windows and sampling.
ops-bot: Added checklist items to ML-4401 and pinged assignees. :memo:

title: #eng-ml: 1717778000
preview:
Ravi Singh: Quick sync - we need clearer rollback/hysteresis rules for low-bit rollouts. current 'fail fast' reverts are noisy for transient noise.
Olivia Park: Agreed. My proposal: add a hysteresis window so a single spike doesn't auto-revert. e.g., require 3 consecutive windows (1m windows) breaching threshold before revert.
Mei Tan: +1. Also add a cool-down after a revert to avoid flip-flop (e.g., 30m hold).
Jordan Blake: We should separate metrics that trigger immediate safety revert (safety-critical degradation) vs those that are soft (quality delta). Immediate metric: safety_token_mismatch or hallucination_count above X -> immediate revert. Soft metric: human-eval proxy (degradation_score) -> hysteresis.
Ravi Singh: Good. Concrete thresholds suggestion: safety metric -> immediate. quality metric -> breach if degradation_rate > 0.6% for 3 windows. latency -> breach if p95 delta > 5ms for 3 windows.
Olivia Park: Also support per-tenant sensitivity: enterprise customers might set tighter thresholds, small tenants looser. Add tenant_override in profile meta.
Mei Tan: Need to define windowing semantics. suggest sliding windows with step = 30s, window_size = 60s, requiring N consecutive breached windows. That smooths short spikes while still being responsive.
Jordan Blake: For revert action, plan two modes: auto-revert (default for non-safety issues) and gated-revert (requires human ack for safety_critical models/tenants). Controlled by profile metadata: auto_revert: true/false.
Ravi Singh: Add provenance tagging to the revert: include profile_id, signer, canary_percent, triggered_metric, window_summary. Keep it compact in alert payload for postmortems.
Olivia Park: Telemetry impact: we mustn't explode cardinality. We'll store aggregated counters per-deploy + sampled detailed traces for breached windows. Keep per-tenant summaries only when tenant has overrides.
Mei Tan: Playbook: when auto-revert happens create an incident with severity based on metric. For soft reverts, auto-create a rollback ticket but tag for triage. Ops-bot?
ops-bot: Created ML-4401: "Quant rollback hysteresis + playbook" assigned to Ravi Singh, Olivia Park, Mei Tan, Jordan Blake. https://redwood.atlassian.com/browse/ML-4401 :white_check_mark:
Jordan Blake: We need a lint step that prevents profiles from setting auto_revert=true combined with aggressive thresholds for safety_critical ops. Block if unsafe.
Ravi Singh: Good call. Also add a manual "hold" flag to temporarily disable auto-revert for debugging canaries. Admin-only.
Olivia Park: UI needs to show current holograph of hysteresis state per-deploy (OK / warning (1/3) / breached (3/3) / reverted). That helps SREs see progress.
Mei Tan: For postmortems we want a compact event line: "ts, deploy, profile_id, tenant, metric, windows=[0.2,0.7,0.8], action=revert". That makes search easy.
Jordan Blake: How about a graceful degrade path instead of full revert? e.g., step back from fp8->int8 globally instead of full rollback to model-level. That can buy time while ML investigates.
Ravi Singh: I like staged rollback options: revert_to_previous_signed_profile, fallback_level_one (e.g., move to next entry in fallback chain), or full-disable-operator_level. Profile should define preferred rollback modality.
Olivia Park: Security: ensure revert commands are audited and require 2FA for manual overrides on prod.
Mei Tan: Implementation items: 1) runtime: sliding-window aggregator + hysteresis state machine. 2) telemetry: compact window summaries + sampled traces. 3) ML: mapping from degradation_score -> fallback severity. 4) product: UI states and manual hold button.
Ravi Singh: Timeline? want PoC for hysteresis state machine in two weeks.
Jordan Blake: I'll prototype the state machine and staged rollback hooks this week.
Olivia Park: I'll draft the UI states and incident templates.
Mei Tan: I'll implement telemetry windows and sampling.
ops-bot: Added checklist items to ML-4401 and pinged assignees. :memo:

source.thread:
Nadia Bose: Heads-up: planning int3 bring-up next week. Need a promptset that stresses tokenization edgecases, deid leaks, repeated-prefix KV-cache, and code diffs. Can curation produce a mixed real+synth slice for the canary?
Ethan Park: Yup. Proposal: 60% real / 40% synth, with ~20% adversarial paraphrases so we get hard negatives for hallucination-F1.
Nadia Bose: Also want a golden set of 100 examples stratified: 35 deid, 35 longform/multi-turn, 30 code/RAG. Golden should be label-ready with rubric.
Mei Wong: I can own the golden. Will reserve labeler capacity and provide 8 rubric examples per holdout. Will we dedupe against samplebank v3?
Ethan Park: Yes, de-dupe against samplebank-v3 and mark provenance fields: `synth_seed:20260322`, `synth_generator:para-v3`, `pii_masked:true`, `kvcache_repeat:1|3`. I'll emit a manifest CSV.
ops-bot: created ticket INF-5272 for "int3 canary promptset + golden 100" - https://tracker/INF-5272
Nadia Bose: When you run the quant smoke, run this and attach outputs to INF-5272:
```
./quant-smoke --model int3-candidate --seed 20260322 --holdouts=deid,longform,code --out logs/int3-smoke-$(date +%s).json
```
Collect per-holdout LLR, hallucination-F1 (deid prioritized), token-split rate, and tokenizer-diff.tsv. Prioritize deid halluc-F1 over BLEU.
Mei Wong: I'll draft the labeler rubric for PII leak vs acceptable redaction and upload a tiny golden scoring sheet. Target dry-run by Thursday.
Ethan Park: Will keep generator deterministic and include `adversarial:true` tags on paraphrases. PR with manifests + small sample coming in ~3 hours.
Nadia Bose: Thanks. If deid halluc-F1 drops >10pts vs baseline, we abort the canary and revert routing to int3-stable.

source.messages:
Ethan Park: Heads-up idea: for Dedicated/Private customers we should sign quant profiles (profile provenance) so runtime can verify origin and prevent accidental unsafe overrides.
Nina Gomez: signing sounds good for compliance. What would we sign exactly? whole profile blob or a canonical digest?
Ethan Park: canonical digest + metadata (author, calibration_version, signature_time). Keep blobs in artifact store; runtime fetches signed manifest.
Lukas Weber: runtime cost: verifying a signature per-deploy is cheap, but per-request would be too slow. verify at load-time and cache.
Nina Gomez: also need an audit trail: who approved the profile, which eval anchors passed. tie to ML-rollback playbook.
Ethan Park: proposal: profile lifecycle: draft -> signed-for-staging -> staging-eval -> signed-for-production. Each sign step requires lint + anchor checks.
Lukas Weber: deploy-time checks: cert root rotation, signature expiry, and tenant allowlist. If signature invalid, runtime falls back to tenant-default safe profile.
Nina Gomez: product ask: allow customers to bring their own signing key (BYOK) for private. Provide verification hooks for on-prem.
Ethan Park: ops: store short provenance header in request logs: profile_id, signer_email, sig_version. keep it compact to not bloat logs.
Lukas Weber: telemetry: add provenance fields in per-deploy metrics so we can attribute regressions to profile versions. Also postmortem search by signer.
Nina Gomez: security: threat model - signed but malicious profile. Mitigate: require two-person sign for any profile that downgrades precision beyond threshold, and safety_critical ops require manual approval.
Ethan Park: runtime auto-revert rule: if production profile causes anchor-diff > threshold or human-eval drop, auto-revert to previous signed profile and alert.
ops-bot: Created ticket ML-4310: "Quant profile provenance & signing flow" assigned to Ethan Park, Nina Gomez, Lukas Weber. https://redwood.atlassian.com/browse/ML-4310 :white_check_mark:
Nina Gomez: next steps: 1) spec manifest format + signing fields (Nina). 2) runtime: load-time verify + cache + auto-revert (Lukas). 3) ML: lint/signing gates + two-person approval (Ethan).
Ethan Park: timeline: spec this week, PoC load-time verify next sprint. Good meeting, thanks.
